title: System Wiki Articles — Agent Bootstrap Set

story.as_a: new agent or integration developer who has just fetched the discovery endpoint
story.i_want_to: read the canonical articles that explain how loopctl's trust model works at a conceptual level, how agents participate in it, what happens at tenant signup, and what the minimum viable bootstrap flow looks like
story.so_that: I can understand the system without reading source code, follow a worked example from first contact to verified work, and recover from trust-model errors with actionable instructions

rationale: The discovery endpoint (US-26.0.4) and every trust-model error response links to system articles that MUST exist with real, accurate content. Empty stubs or placeholder articles would break the self-discovery contract and leave agents stuck. This story authors the foundational article set that bootstraps a new agent into the platform. The articles are long-lived authoritative references — they describe stable concepts (trust model overview, agent lifecycle, signup flow) that don't change per release. Subsequent docs-content stories (US-26.3.3, US-26.4.5) cover phase-specific articles.

acceptance_criteria.description: System article at slug `chain-of-custody` exists with scope `:system`. Content: 1500-3000 words explaining the trust model at a conceptual level (the two threat profiles, the six-layer enforcement stack, the rationale behind structural-over-policy enforcement). Includes at least one diagram rendered as ASCII or via a simple SVG embedded in markdown. Links to all other articles in this set.

System article at slug `agent-bootstrap` exists. Content: a step-by-step walkthrough for a brand-new agent from first contact (fetching /.well-known/loopctl) to the first successful API call (listing ready stories). Includes an actual curl-based worked example with placeholder UUIDs and a commentary on what each step does and why. This is the article the discovery endpoint points at as `discovery_bootstrap_url`.

System article at slug `agent-pattern` exists. Content: describes the expected agent lifecycle (discover → dispatch → claim → work → report → wait → learn from re-review if rejected). Includes diagrams of the state machine, example MCP tool calls for each transition, and pointers to the specific articles that explain each step's trust-model requirements.

System article at slug `tenant-signup` exists. Content: explains the human-operator signup ceremony, WebAuthn requirements, what happens when you enroll an authenticator, how to add backup authenticators, how the audit-signing keypair is generated and stored, and how tenant recovery works if all authenticators are lost. Written for a human operator, not an agent.

System article at slug `discovery` exists. Content: the .well-known/loopctl contract — the response schema, how URL fields are stable across deployments, how to use the document to construct subsequent URLs, how to cache it, and what changes when `spec_version` bumps.
Every article in this set is tagged with categories drawn from the existing knowledge wiki taxonomy (pattern, convention, decision, finding, reference). These articles are all `reference` category.

Articles cross-link using stable slug URLs like `/wiki/agent-bootstrap`. No broken internal links (verified by the link-checker from US-26.0.3). External links use full `https://` URLs to spec documents (RFCs, WebAuthn spec) and are validated to return 200 at article publish time.

Articles are authored in Markdown and stored as seed data in a migration (`priv/repo/migrations/<ts>_seed_epic_26_phase_0_articles.exs`). The seed uses the system-scope article path (US-26.0.3). Re-running the migration is idempotent (UPSERT on slug).
Each article includes a `last_updated` field in its frontmatter pointing at the epic/phase that authored it (`epic_26_phase_0`). Later updates bump this field and reference the updating story.

Articles include at least one worked example using realistic placeholder data (fake UUIDs, fake tenant slugs). Examples are tagged with the `phx-no-curly-interpolation` attribute where they contain literal `{` or `}` in JSON/HEEx snippets.

A CI-level check verifies that every error response in the API with a `learn_more` URL actually resolves to a real article (either from this story or a stub slug reserved by a previous story). The check runs as part of `mix precommit`.

test_cases.name: All five articles exist after the seed migration runs
steps: Query articles table for scope = system
Check for each of the 5 slugs
expected_results: 5 system articles exist with slugs: chain-of-custody, agent-bootstrap, agent-pattern, tenant-signup, discovery

test_cases.name: Articles render at /wiki/:slug without errors
expected_results: Each response body is valid HTML with rendered markdown content
No broken internal link warnings in any response

test_cases.name: Cross-links between articles resolve correctly
steps: Parse the HTML for internal links
For each internal link, GET it
expected_results: Every /wiki/:slug link points at a real article

test_cases.name: Bootstrap walkthrough is reproducible end-to-end
steps: Parse the article body for curl examples
Execute the first curl example with a real api_key (substituting placeholders)
expected_results: The curl example executes successfully with a real response

test_cases.name: Seed migration is idempotent
expected_results: Article bodies are unchanged or updated if the migration body was updated

test_cases.name: Link-check test flags broken internal links
preconditions: Test inserts a system article with a broken /wiki/:slug link
expected_results: Output includes the broken slug and the article that references it

technical_notes: Writing Markdown articles is a significant part of this story — estimate at least 8 hours of research and drafting for the five articles together. Prioritize clarity and accuracy over prose polish.

Author articles as Elixir heredoc strings in the seed migration file: `@chain_of_custody_body """
# Chain of Custody
...
"""`. Heredocs preserve Markdown formatting.
Category mapping: all 5 articles are category `reference` (stable authoritative content). In the future, pattern/decision/finding categories may be used for other article types.
Seed migration uses `repo.insert!/1` with ON CONFLICT (slug) UPDATE for idempotency. Use the existing Article context function if available.

The link-check script is a Mix task `mix loopctl.check_wiki_links` that crawls all system articles, extracts `/wiki/:slug` references, and verifies each slug exists. Exits 0 on success, non-zero on failure.
Hook the link-check into `mix precommit` by adding it to the precommit alias in mix.exs.

For the worked examples, use these placeholder UUIDs: tenant_id = `00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001`, agent_id = `00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002`, story_id = `00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000003`. Mark them clearly as placeholders in the prose.
Diagrams: ASCII art is fine. For more complex diagrams, use inline SVG embedded in Markdown (Earmark passes through HTML). Keep diagrams simple — avoid Mermaid or other live-rendering deps.
Each article's body starts with a one-paragraph summary that serves as the search snippet, followed by a full section breakdown.

The five articles cross-reference each other via `/wiki/:slug` relative URLs. Do NOT use absolute URLs (https://loopctl.com/wiki/...) internally — keep them relative so dev/test environments work too.
Reference: docs/chain-of-custody-v2.md for all technical content. The articles are user-facing rewrites of sections from the design doc.
